text: Welcome to another installment of robotic inguinal hernia surgery. Today, we'll review a case performed in a woman who presented with an inguinal hernia and possible femoral hernia.

This was a sixty-five year old woman presenting with a left groin bulge. She had a history of hypertension, multiple pelvic procedures, had a reducible inguinal hernia, no symptoms on the contralateral side.
Before our encounter, she had already had an ultrasound with a possible femoral identified.
The surgeon begins the operation by developing a peritoneal flap, carefully lifting and separating the peritoneum from underlying structures.
The transabdominal view reveals an indirect hernia and visible weakness in the femoral area. The surgeon begins developing the standard peritoneal flap.
The surgeon carefully maintains the preperitoneal flap, ensuring not to penetrate into the pre-transversalis layer during the dissection.
And then when I start developing my preperitoneal dissection, I really try to take advantage of the various planes well described by Dr. David Laurier.

Now here, I'm in the visceral compartment. A lot of times people will talk about that medial dissection being in the parietal compartment. My initial dissection is visceral. My eventual space will be parietal. I'll show you how I do that. Now lateral, definitely working to stay in that visceral compartment. This is not yet visceral. You'll see us
The surgeon transitions into the visceral compartment, taking down the intermediate fascia. Fatty and areolar tissues are reflected towards the lateral sidewall.
The surgeon focuses on reducing the small indirect hernia by grasping the hernia sac and pulling it to facilitate reduction.
The surgeon carefully extracts the hernia sac from the canal, ensuring that no additional tissue is inadvertently removed along with it.

The surgeon begins developing the critical view of the myopectineal orifice, focusing on the intermediate fascia. The area above the bladder is identified as the entry point into the parietal compartment.
The intermediate fascia is visible, providing protection to small branches off the epigastric vessels and around the retropubic space, safeguarding the bladder from potential injury.
The surgeon examines various anatomical spaces for potential hernias, focusing on identifying femoral defects. A small fat-containing femoral hernia is visually identified during the dissection.
The surgeon reduces the hernia contents, creating a flat area in preparation for mesh placement.
We will continuously jump back and forth. It's one of the things I do. I just go back to where it's easy and work on developing my critical view.
The surgeon develops a lateral pocket and identifies the round ligament, preparing to transect it.

The round ligament is often described as being taken in these cases. I wholeheartedly agree with that. It serves no functional role and further aids in allowing us to lay our mesh as flat as possible in this space.
The surgeon identifies the round ligament and uses energy devices to transect it, ensuring hemostasis due to the presence of small vessels that may bleed.
The surgeon transects the round ligament close to the peritoneum, minimizing the risk of injury to nearby nerves around the internal ring.

The surgeon performs further retropubic dissection, ensuring at least two centimeters of contralateral dissection, while avoiding exposure of the contralateral Cooper's ligament due to the absence of a direct hernia.
The surgeon tidies up the dissection area, ensuring a clear and satisfactory critical view of the surgical field.
The surgeon prepares to place a mid-weight polypropylene mesh to address a small femoral defect and a relatively small indirect hernia.

The surgeon achieves good coverage of the mesh prosthesis overlay (MPO) and proceeds with fixation for a femoral hernia. A single stitch is placed into Cooper's ligament, visible on the right side, followed by another stitch onto the lacunar ligament.
The surgeon closes the flap from lateral to medial, ensuring minimal tension on the flap during the closure process.

At this point, my insufflation pressure has dropped down to between six and eight. And we'll just work on a very simple flap closure here. No issues with operation. Patient did great. And that's my approach to
some of the differences in management of inguinal hernias in women versus men. Thank you very much.
